glossary.def: The empirical regularity that large asset-price changes tend to be followed by large changes (of either sign) and small by small, so periods of turbulence and calm arrive in bunches. Formally, returns are nearly uncorrelated while squared or absolute returns show strong, slowly-decaying positive autocorrelation.

A time-varying variance that depends on the past — the variance of the next observation is predictable from previous data even when the observation's level is not. It is the property classical constant-variance (homoscedastic) models cannot represent and the entire ARCH/GARCH family was built to capture.

Engle's 1982 Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity model, in which today's conditional variance is a constant plus a weighted sum of recent squared shocks. It captures clustering directly but needs many lags to express realistic persistence, motivating its GARCH generalization.

Bollerslev's 1986 model that sets today's conditional variance to omega + alpha·(yesterday's squared shock) + beta·(yesterday's variance). With just three parameters it captures slowly-decaying volatility persistence and is equivalent to an infinite exponentially-weighted ARCH, making it the field's default workhorse.

In GARCH(1,1), the sum of the reaction and memory coefficients, governing how slowly a volatility shock decays back toward its long-run level. Values near 1 (typical for daily equity indices, 0.95–0.99) mean shocks fade only over many sessions; at exactly 1 the model becomes IGARCH and the unconditional variance is undefined.

The asymmetry in equity volatility whereby a negative return raises future volatility more than a positive return of equal size. Plain GARCH squares the shock and so ignores its sign; GJR-GARCH adds a term that switches on only for down days, and EGARCH models log-variance with a sign-dependent news term.

A plot of next period's conditional variance against the current shock, holding the prior variance fixed. Symmetric GARCH traces a parabola centred at zero; asymmetric models (GJR, EGARCH) tilt it so the loss (left) arm rises more steeply, visualizing the leverage effect.

A loss threshold a portfolio will not exceed with a given probability over a horizon, computed as minus the mean plus the lower-tail quantile of the innovation distribution times the GARCH conditional volatility. Because the volatility is conditional it widens automatically in turbulent regimes and tightens in calm ones, unlike a static historical VaR that lags the regime.

refs.cite: Engle, R. F. (1982). Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity with Estimates of the Variance of United Kingdom Inflation. Econometrica 50(4).
Bollerslev, T. (1986). Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity. Journal of Econometrics 31(3).
Nelson, D. B. (1991). Conditional Heteroskedasticity in Asset Returns: A New Approach. Econometrica 59(2).
Glosten, L. R., Jagannathan, R. & Runkle, D. E. (1993). On the Relation between the Expected Value and the Volatility of the Nominal Excess Return on Stocks. Journal of Finance 48(5).
Hansen, P. R. & Lunde, A. (2005). A Forecast Comparison of Volatility Models: Does Anything Beat a GARCH(1,1)? Journal of Applied Econometrics 20(7).
Engle, R. F. (2002). Dynamic Conditional Correlation: A Simple Class of Multivariate GARCH Models. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics 20(3).
Mandelbrot, B. (1963). The Variation of Certain Speculative Prices. Journal of Business 36(4).
